topic: Topic 5: Social Activities, Fitness and Health
subject: german

items.q: How do you say 'I use my phone every day' in German?
items.correct: Ich benutze mein Handy jeden Tag.
items.misconception: Ich nutze mein Telefon jeden Tag.
items.random_wrong: Ich gebrauche mein Phone jeden Tag.
items.why: Students use 'Telefon' (which means landline/telephone). 'Handy' is the German word for mobile phone - confusingly looks like an English adjective but is the standard term. Also: BENUTZEN is the most common 'to use'.

items.q: What is the difference between 'ich will' and 'ich möchte'?
items.correct: Ich will = I want (strong, direct); ich möchte = I would like (polite).
items.why: Students learn wollen as 'to want' and use it casually. 'Ich will...' sounds DEMANDING/childish. Polite German uses ICH MÖCHTE (Konjunktiv form of mögen) for 'I would like'. Essential for job/interview contexts.
Spelled identically to English 'bald' but means SOON. 'Bis bald!' = 'See you soon!'. The English 'bald' translates as 'kahl' or 'glatzköpfig'. Pure faux ami.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I have a headache'?
items.random_wrong: Ich habe Schmerzen auf dem Kopf.
items.why: It's Ich habe Kopfschmerzen.. “Ich bin Kopfschmerzen.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence advises 'You should drink more water'?
items.misconception: Du sollst mehr Wasser trinken werden.
items.why: It's Du solltest mehr Wasser trinken.. “Du sollst mehr Wasser trinken werden.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many hours of sleep per night are typically recommended for teenagers?
items.why: Exact answer: 9 hours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
